item.title: ChatGPT for Science Leak, AI Guardrail Gaps, and Executive Phishing Tradecraft
item.description: This digest window is dominated by two related themes: enterprise exposure around AI adoption and realistic social engineering tradecraft against senior leadership. Reporting highlights continued specialization of commercial AI offerings for research environments, while security commentary...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by two related themes: enterprise exposure around AI adoption and realistic social engineering tradecraft against senior leadership. Reporting highlights continued specialization of commercial AI offerings for research environments, while security commentary underscores gaps between provider-native AI controls and organizational SecOps visibility needs. Separately, an authorized assessment shows how public construction details and journalist impersonation can be used to drive executive engagement and downstream third-party exposure. BleepingComputer reported that OpenAI appears to be testing a new subscription and experience called ChatGPT for Science, based on references found in the ChatGPT web build. The article notes that availability remains unclear and that the offering may be restricted to verified institutes or universities rather than broadly available to all users. OpenAI already offers ChatGPT for personal, Teams, business, and enterprise customers, and recently announced GPT-Rosalind, a specialized model for enterprise-scale life sciences research delivered through a trusted-access deployment structure. The reporting reflects a broader trend toward domain-specific AI services for scientific and enterprise research workflows. In the life sciences context, specialized models and restricted deployment structures indicate that vendors are attempting to balance advanced research capability with controlled access models. The article does not state that ChatGPT for Science has been publicly launched, only that references in the web build suggest testing is underway. Netskope separately argued that native AI guardrails from providers such as Amazon Bedrock, Anthropic, and OpenAI often align more closely with provider liability and intellectual property protection than with enterprise SecOps requirements. The article states that these controls may block legitimate cybersecurity research while still allowing sensitive prompts or intellectual property to be sent to third-party providers. It also describes observability gaps where refusals or silent model behavior changes may not clearly identify which guardrail was triggered or why. NetSPI described an authorized social engineering assessment targeting a client’s executive leadership team. Testers used open-source research about a planned new facility to build a journalist impersonation pretext focused on alleged hazardous waste disposal at the construction site. The assessment used a ProtonMail account in a journalist’s name, a lookalike domain, and an Evilginx server designed to mimic the client’s Microsoft login flow and capture authentication data during the test. The scenario gained traction when an executive responsible for the construction project responded to the pretext. After the testers claimed there was a Teams issue, the executive received a phishing link and forwarded it to two contracting firms involved in the project. NetSPI stopped the phishing session and notified its point of contact because those contracting firms were outside the authorized engagement scope. The case illustrates how adversary-style pretexts can combine public business developments, executive responsibilities, and time-sensitive reputational concerns to increase credibility. It also shows how a phishing operation aimed at one organization can quickly propagate into adjacent vendors or contractors when trusted business relationships are involved.

item.title: Malicious VS Code Extensions, Crypto Clippers, CISA BOD 26-04, and Google IP Ad Targeting
item.description: This coverage window centers on trust boundaries that attackers and platform operators are increasingly exploiting or redefining: developer tooling, reputation systems, vulnerability prioritization, and user-identifying network metadata. The most security-relevant items involve malicious editor...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window centers on trust boundaries that attackers and platform operators are increasingly exploiting or redefining: developer tooling, reputation systems, vulnerability prioritization, and user-identifying network metadata. The most security-relevant items involve malicious editor extensions reaching privileged developer environments and a crypto clipper campaign using coordinated fake legitimacy signals across software-hosting and media platforms. Socket announced beta support in Socket Firewall for blocking malicious VS Code Marketplace and Open VSX extensions before they reach developer workstations. The capability routes editor marketplace requests through Socket’s proxy and filters search, install, update, and VSIX download flows based on threat verdicts, extending supply chain controls into IDE extension ecosystems. The announcement cites GitHub’s May 2026 disclosure that attackers compromised an employee device through the malicious Nx Console 18.95.0 VS Code extension and exfiltrated roughly 3,800 GitHub-internal repositories. Socket emphasized that editor extensions may access source code, terminals, files, credentials, tokens, and internal systems, making compromised extensions a high-impact intrusion path inside development environments. Check Point Research reported a crypto clipper campaign distributing a Rust-based clipboard hijacker disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots and crash-game predictors. The malware targets Windows and macOS users, monitors clipboard contents for cryptocurrency wallet address patterns, and replaces matching entries with attacker-controlled addresses from a hard-coded list. The campaign’s distribution model relies heavily on manufactured credibility. The actor used paid or promoted posts on legitimate news websites, coordinated positive comments on VirusTotal, GitHub, SourceForge, and YouTube, and inflated indicators such as stars, forks, downloads, upvotes, and AI-generated narrator content. Check Point also reported use of EIN Presswire to market the tools’ claimed capabilities, resulting in syndication across partner news sites, primarily in the USA TODAY Network. Tenable published analysis on operationalizing CISA Binding Operational Directive 26-04, which supersedes BOD 19-02 and BOD 22-01. The directive shifts federal vulnerability management away from static severity scoring and toward remediation timelines based on four contextual risk variables: asset exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities status, exploit automation, and technical impact. According to Tenable, asset exposure is the highest-leverage variable in the directive’s model. Its analysis found that removing public exposure shifted 76.7% of CVEs from compressed remediation timelines into the deferral tier. The article also notes that BOD 26-04 introduces a forensic triage requirement for KEV-listed vulnerabilities that yield total system control, and Tenable states that 83% of actively exploited CVEs meet that impact threshold. Google has begun notifying advertisers that it will start using IP addresses for ad measurement and personalization across the European Economic Area, the UK, and Switzerland on or shortly after August 3, 2026. The company said IP addresses it already receives for traffic routing and ad delivery will also be used to identify devices for measurement and personalization. The article notes that IP addresses are considered personal data under GDPR in the EEA, the UK, and Switzerland, and that using automatically transmitted information such as IP addresses to identify devices is a building block of fingerprinting. Google plans to register under IAB Europe’s Transparency and Consent Framework for Feature 3, while its customer email states that advertisers remain bound by Google’s EU User Consent Policy and must obtain valid consent from users in affected regions.

item.title: ClickFix SmartRAT and Meow Campaigns, FortiBleed Leak, and UK CNI State Threats
item.description: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting,...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting, and renewed warnings about state prepositioning in critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported a March 2026 ClickFix campaign using an AI-generated typosquatting site impersonating a Brazilian bank to deliver SmartRAT. The lure presented a fraudulent Cloudflare CAPTCHA followed by a fullscreen fake BSOD recovery prompt, pressuring victims into executing a PowerShell command that downloaded the malware. SmartRAT provides encrypted C2, remote screen and input control, keylogging, fake bank overlays, QR code interception, and persistence via scheduled tasks and a Windows service. SmartRAT is financially focused, monitoring window titles associated with Brazilian banking, payment, and cryptocurrency activity and alerting operators when victims interact with targeted services. ThreatLabz also found a weakness in the SmartRAT web C2 panel in which client-side authentication could be bypassed by setting arbitrary localStorage values. Netskope Threat Labs separately described a macOS ClickFix campaign observed on May 31, 2026 that delivered an upgraded AppleScript payload labeled “Meow (DEBUG).” The infection chain remained fileless until persistence, using a victim-executed curl command to fetch a compressed stager and pipe AppleScript into osascript memory. The payload checks for Russian keyboard input, steals credentials, cookies, keychain contents, crypto wallet data, messaging sessions, files, gaming data, and Apple Notes, then persists as com.apple.accountsd for recurring C2 beacons and arbitrary command execution. A newly identified leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed apparent Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs worldwide. Researcher Bob Diachenko said the exposed server contained usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords, with references to major organizations including Chevron, Samsung, Foxconn, Comcast, AT&T, Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Sinopec, and State Grid. Kevin Beaumont reported confirming the authenticity of some administrative credentials and said the dataset appeared to originate from exported Fortinet configurations. The FortiBleed reporting attributed the activity to a Russian-speaking, multi-operator threat group that allegedly made about 1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 FortiGate targets and 2.1 billion attempts against 163,650 Microsoft SQL Server systems. In parallel, Zscaler’s 2026 phishing reporting found that overall phishing volume in its cloud fell 20% year over year, while remaining campaigns became more targeted, AI-powered, and difficult to distinguish from legitimate activity. Zscaler identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances during the analysis period and assessed 9% as malicious. Government phishing attempts increased 50% year over year to 138.5 million hits in 2025, while healthcare saw lower phishing counts but 95.2% of phishing activity over encrypted channels. Separate account takeover reporting emphasized attacker adaptation to MFA through fatigue tactics, adversary-in-the-middle frameworks, browser-in-the-middle kits, session hijacking, and credential theft from unmanaged or compromised endpoints. Richard Horne, chief executive of the UK National Cyber Security Centre, said the agency handled more than 200 incidents affecting UK critical infrastructure and its supporting ecosystem in the year to May, with roughly 75% believed to involve state actors. He warned that adversaries are prepositioning inside technology underpinning British critical national infrastructure to enable rapid exploitation and mass disruption during conflict. Horne cited Volt Typhoon as the clearest example of this prepositioning model. The remarks coincided with UK government work on the Cyber Security and Resilience Bill and an expected National Cyber Action Plan. Horne framed cybersecurity as a contest against capable opponents rather than only a risk-management discipline, emphasizing capability relative to adversaries. Ukraine has also been granted access to the European Union’s Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing Kyiv to request EU-approved expertise during major cyberattacks. The reserve, managed by ENISA, provides incident response, digital forensics, technical expertise, and recovery support when incidents exceed national capacity. Ukrainian officials said the country already shares intelligence on Russian hacking techniques with European partners and participates in joint cybercrime investigations and attribution work. Censys analyzed AdaptixC2, an open-source C2 framework whose default configuration exposes identifiable HTTP headers such as Server: AdaptixC2 and Adaptix-Version: v1.2 on unauthenticated error responses. As of 16 June 2026, Censys tracked 412 web properties across 236 hosts using default or near-default AdaptixC2 settings. The research identified beacon listener clusters hosted by Hivelocity, M247, and PSB Hosting, as well as one host serving AdaptixC2 payloads from an open HTTP directory. The exposed directory included a Linux installer that deployed timesync.bin and established 12 persistence mechanisms. Censys noted that even operators who changed the default Server header could remain detectable if they left the default 404 response body string intact. Cato Networks reported on a French-speaking attacker known as “Poisson,” who compromised a small French automotive business, installed a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials. Before his Havoc C2 server went offline, the attacker installed OpenSSH Server and Tailscale on a victim machine to preserve access outside the original C2 channel. Cato captured 339 commands over 33 days after the operator left SSH keys and a step-by-step playbook in an open storage bucket, with the intrusion involving VBScript, PowerShell, .NET loaders, Havoc Demon, scheduled-task persistence, Explorer.exe injection, RustDesk, and a Python keylogger. Silent Push described the preparation phase as the period in which threat actors build and stage domains, servers, DNS records, certificates, and testing environments before a campaign becomes active. The company argued that SIEMs, EDR platforms, blocklists, and standard threat intelligence feeds generally rely on observed malicious events or known-bad indicators, leaving this phase underdetected. The article stated that traditional threat data captures roughly the final 5% of the offensive lifecycle, when infrastructure is already operational. Silent Push said its approach maps DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, and host configurations to identify Indicators of Future Attack, claiming an average early-detection lead time of 104 days ahead of traditional SIEM alerts and more than 300 days in some APT cases.

item.title: Mastra npm Takeover, CVE-2026-48907 Exploitation, and Crypto Hijacker Campaigns
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and productivity platforms, alongside ongoing concerns about exposure prioritization and public-sector cyber capacity. Mend reported a compromise of more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope, where an attacker republished packages with a new dependency on easy-day-js. Version 1.11.22 of easy-day-js introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper that retrieved a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000 and launched it as a detached hidden process communicating with 23.254.164.123:443. The second stage was described as harvesting environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, with affected installations treated in the reporting as fully compromised. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address CVE-2026-48907, a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin. The JCE security team said the vulnerability allows unauthenticated PHP upload and execution, is actively exploited, has public exploit code available, and is being targeted through automated attacks. The flaw was fixed in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6, while the vendor noted that updating does not remediate sites already compromised before patching. Broader exposure data from Intruder underscored why these classes of issues remain operationally significant. Across 3,000 attack surfaces, 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed, 42% had an internet-reachable database, and 30% exposed files or information that should not have been public. MySQL, Postgres, API documentation, WordPress admin panels, and RDP were among the most common findings. Check Point Research analyzed a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijacker campaign disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor tools, and crash-game predictors. The actor used a WordPress phishing site as a landing hub and promoted payloads through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites. The campaign also inflated trust signals using coordinated or fake GitHub engagement, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube activity, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments. The Windows payload persisted by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, while the macOS variant used an unlocker.command script to remove quarantine attributes and installed a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive. Both monitored clipboard or pasteboard contents and replaced cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses. IRONSCALES reported a French-language invoice notification sent through Mailgun from an Ecocert accounting subdomain that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication. The email linked to an Upflow billing portal URL containing a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration, allowing anyone with the tokenized link to access the portal without credentials. The invoice requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC details, while a tracking pixel confirmed mailbox activity. BleepingComputer reported that India restricted Telegram access nationwide until June 22 after the National Testing Agency said the platform was used to sell access to leaked exam materials. The disruption extended beyond India, including to users in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked globally via FLAG Telecom. Researchers confirmed the route hijack but said they found no evidence that the wider disruption was deliberate, while civil society groups criticized the block as disproportionate. Microsoft confirmed it is investigating Office launch issues after Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026. The issue affects third-party applications using OLE automation to open Office apps or documents, including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Access, and may fail without an error message. User reports named affected applications such as CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, and Softdent. Operationally, The Record covered Sen. Mark Warner’s letter warning of CISA staffing cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators. Separately, Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program for managed SSE and SASE delivery, while a Hacker News article framed adversarial exposure validation as a way to distinguish theoretical exposure from reachable and consequential risk within Continuous Threat Exposure Management.

item.title: Digest 2026-06-17 07:29 UTC
item.content:encoded:
FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices. — Must Read

A leaked dataset dubbed FortiBleed appears to expose valid Fortinet/FortiGate VPN and admin credentials for about 74,000 firewall URLs worldwide, including major enterprises and government-linked organizations. Affected organizations should immediately rotate Fortinet passwords, enable MFA, and review logs for compromise.

  • A newly discovered data leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed what appears to be Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations worldwide.
  • Security researcher Bob Diachenko said he found a server containing usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords, with entries referencing companies such as Chevron, Samsung, Foxconn, Comcast, AT&T, Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Sinopec, and State Grid.
  • Diachenko claimed the operation was conducted by a Russian-speaking multi-operator threat group that made about 1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 FortiGate targets and 2.1 billion attempts against 163,650 Microsoft SQL Server systems.
  • Cybersecurity researcher Kevin Beaumont said he confirmed the authenticity of some admin logins and passwords and found the dataset appears to have originated from exported Fortinet configurations.
  • Organizations in the dataset are advised to rotate Fortinet VPN and administrative passwords, enforce MFA, examine gateway logs for suspicious activity, and monitor for exposed employee credentials.

macOS ClickFix Lures Deploy AppleScript Stealer & Persistent RAT — Must Read

Netskope found a new macOS ClickFix campaign that tricks users into running a fileless command, then deploys the “Meow” AppleScript stealer plus a persistent RAT. The malware steals credentials, browser and wallet data, modifies desktop crypto wallet apps, and installs a fake Apple accounts service for ongoing remote command execution.

  • Netskope Threat Labs observed a new macOS ClickFix campaign on May 31, 2026 that delivers an upgraded AppleScript-based payload self-labeled “Meow (DEBUG)” with remote access trojan capabilities.
  • The infection chain is fileless until persistence is established, with victims socially engineered into running a curl command that fetches a gzip-compressed stager and pipes a second-stage AppleScript directly into osascript memory.
  • The stage 1 loader checks the macOS keyboard layout for Russian input in com.apple.HIToolbox.plist, reports a cis_blocked event to the C2 if Russian input is found, and exits silently.
  • The payload steals the user’s login password through a fake System Preferences dialog and collects browser credentials, cookies, keychain contents, crypto wallet data, messaging sessions, gaming account data, files, and Apple Notes.
  • After exfiltration, the malware can overwrite app.asar files in selected desktop cryptocurrency wallets and installs a LaunchDaemon or LaunchAgent labeled com.apple.accountsd that beacons to the C2 every 60 seconds for arbitrary command execution.

Mastra npm Scope Takeover: 140+ Packages Compromised via easy-day-js Dropper — Must Read

An attacker compromised the @mastra npm scope by republishing 140+ packages with a malicious easy-day-js dependency that runs a postinstall dropper to steal secrets and crypto wallets. Any machine or CI system that installed affected packages should be treated as fully compromised, cleaned for persistence, and have credentials rotated.

  • An attacker republished more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope with a single added dependency, easy-day-js, and the malicious versions were observed on 2026-06-17.
  • The easy-day-js package version 1.11.22 added an obfuscated postinstall dropper, while version 1.11.21 was a clean prior release with no install hook.
  • Installing an affected @mastra package resolves easy-day-js ^1.11.21 to the malicious 1.11.22 version, causing the postinstall script to run automatically during installation.
  • The dropper downloads a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000/update/49890878 and launches it as a detached, hidden process that communicates with 23.254.164.123:443.
  • The second stage harvests environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, and the article says any machine that installed an affected package should be treated as fully compromised.

Over 140 popular Mastra npm Packages Hit by Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

A supply chain attack compromised 141 packages in the @mastra npm scope by adding a malicious easy-day-js dependency that executed a postinstall payload. The malware downloaded a second-stage RAT, established persistence, and targeted browser-based crypto wallet extensions, making affected installs potentially compromised.

  • A supply chain attack targeted the entire @mastra npm scope, with 141 packages republished between 01:15 and 02:00 UTC on June 17.
  • The compromised packages added a dependency on easy-day-js, a malicious clone of dayjs whose version 1.11.22 included a postinstall hook that ran an obfuscated setup.cjs script.
  • The postinstall script fetched a second-stage payload from 23[.]254[.]164[.]92:8000, wrote it to a random JavaScript file in the OS temp directory, spawned it as a detached background process, and then self-deleted.
  • The second-stage payload collected system information, targeted over 160 browser-based crypto wallet extensions, and established persistence on macOS, Windows, and Linux.
  • The article says the attack closely resembled the earlier axios compromise, including the use of a malicious dependency, a clean decoy version before the malicious release, postinstall execution, and self-deleting droppers.

CISA orders feds to patch max severity Joomla plugin flaw by Friday — Must Read

CISA has ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch an actively exploited maximum-severity Joomla Content Editor plugin flaw, CVE-2026-48907, by Friday. The bug allows unauthenticated attackers to upload and execute PHP code, and sites compromised before patching must be cleaned manually.

  • CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure systems affected by CVE-2026-48907 by Friday under Binding Operational Directive 26-04.
  • CVE-2026-48907 is a maximum-severity improper access control vulnerability in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin that can allow unauthenticated attackers to upload and execute PHP code.
  • The JCE security team fixed the vulnerability in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6 and warned that it is actively exploited, public exploit code is available, and attacks are automated.
  • The JCE security team said updating closes the entry point but does not clean sites that were compromised before patching.
  • Users of compromised sites are advised to back up rogue profiles, update to JCE 2.9.99.6 or later, delete the attacker’s profile, change all passwords, and run a full server-side malware scan.

ClickFix Campaign Generated Via AI Delivers SmartRAT — Must Read

Zscaler ThreatLabz found an AI-generated ClickFix phishing site impersonating a Brazilian bank that tricks users into running PowerShell and installs SmartRAT, a banking RAT with remote control, keylogging, fake bank overlays, QR-code interception, and persistence. The campaign shows how AI-built lures can rapidly enable convincing malware delivery at scale.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz observed a March 2026 ClickFix campaign using an AI-generated typosquatting website impersonating a Brazilian bank to deliver the PowerShell-based SmartRAT malware.
  • The fake website displayed a fraudulent Cloudflare CAPTCHA and then a fullscreen fake BSOD recovery prompt to pressure victims into running a PowerShell command that downloaded and executed the malware.
  • SmartRAT supports encrypted command-and-control communications, remote screen and input control, credential theft through keylogging and fake bank-branded overlays, QR code interception, and persistence through scheduled tasks and a Windows service.
  • SmartRAT monitors window titles for Brazilian banking, payment, and cryptocurrency-related activity and can alert the operator when a victim interacts with targeted financial services.
  • ThreatLabz found that the SmartRAT web-based C2 panel had a client-side authentication weakness that allowed the login screen to be bypassed by setting arbitrary localStorage values.

From Stars to Upvotes: Fake Reputation Fueling a Crypto Clipboard Hijacker — Must Read

Check Point researchers found a Rust-based crypto clipboard hijacker distributed through fake “trading” and gambling tools, with the operator using inflated GitHub/SourceForge engagement, AI YouTube videos, news-site posts, and VirusTotal upvotes to make the malware look legitimate. The campaign targets Windows and macOS users and replaces copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled ones to steal funds.

  • Check Point Research analyzed a campaign distributing Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijackers disguised as tools such as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor, and crash-game predictors.
  • The threat actor used a WordPress phishing site as a central landing page and promoted the malicious tools through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites.
  • The campaign inflated trust signals with fake or coordinated engagement, including GitHub stars and forks, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube views and comments, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments.
  • The Windows payload persists by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, then monitors the clipboard and replaces cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses.
  • The macOS payload uses an “unlocker.command” script to remove quarantine attributes and installs a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive to maintain persistence while monitoring and replacing wallet addresses in the pasteboard.

Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns — Should Read

The UK’s cyber chief warned that hostile states are behind about three-quarters of cyber incidents affecting Britain’s critical infrastructure, with adversaries already prepositioning for potential future conflict. He urged organizations to treat cybersecurity as an ongoing contest against capable opponents, not just a risk-management exercise.

  • Richard Horne, chief executive of the National Cyber Security Centre, said the agency handled more than 200 incidents affecting UK critical infrastructure and its supporting ecosystem in the year to May, with about 75% believed to be caused by state actors.
  • Horne warned that adversaries are prepositioning within technology underpinning British critical national infrastructure to enable rapid exploitation and mass disruption during a conflict.
  • Horne cited Volt Typhoon, a Chinese state-linked campaign exposed against U.S. infrastructure, as the clearest example of this prepositioning tactic.
  • Horne argued that cybersecurity should be treated as a contest to be fought rather than a risk to be managed, saying the relevant benchmark is capability against an opponent rather than industry peers.
  • The speech came as the UK government moves to pass the Cyber Security and Resilience Bill and plans to introduce a new National Cyber Action Plan expected in early July.

India's Telegram ban hit the UAE too. Here's how to get around it — Should Read

India temporarily banned Telegram over alleged NEET exam-leak activity, and a misconfigured BGP route tied to Reliance Communications appears to have disrupted Telegram access beyond India, including in the UAE. Affected users can restore access with Telegram’s built-in MTProto proxy feature, though proxy operators may still see connection metadata.

  • India’s Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology restricted access to Telegram nationwide until June 22 under Section 69A of the IT Act after the National Testing Agency said Telegram was used to sell access to leaked exam materials.
  • Telegram CEO Pavel Durov alleged that Reliance was sabotaging Telegram access outside India through BGP hijacking, while network researchers confirmed AS18101 hijacked Telegram routes but said there was no evidence the disruption was deliberate.
  • The routing leak affected users outside India, including in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked to the global internet via FLAG Telecom.
  • The Internet Freedom Foundation said the nationwide Telegram block was disproportionate and constitutionally incompatible because it affected more than 150 million users to address fraud committed by a smaller group.
  • The article says affected users can restore Telegram access by configuring the app’s built-in MTProto proxy feature with a trusted proxy server, port, and secret.

Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats — Should Read

Researchers found 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins posing as AI coding assistants that steal users’ AI provider API keys, likely to resell access for LLMjacking. Separately, two Chrome ad blocker extensions were caught secretly collecting users’ private conversations with major AI chatbots.

  • Aikido Security reported a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 malicious plugins that pose as AI coding assistants and exfiltrate AI provider API keys.
  • The malicious JetBrains plugins function as advertised but secretly send entered API keys for providers such as OpenAI, SiliconFlow, or DeepSeek to the attacker-controlled server 39.107.60[.]51 over plaintext HTTP.
  • Two of the malicious JetBrains plugins, CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist, have more than 25,000 downloads each, though the article says it is unclear whether the counts are authentic or inflated.
  • Researcher Jean-Marie R. reported that two Chrome ad blocker extensions, Smart Adblocker and Adblock for Browser, capture users’ conversations with AI chatbots including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity, DeepSeek, Grok, and Meta AI.
  • The Chrome extensions provide genuine ad-blocking functionality while running an undisclosed telemetry channel that collects full AI conversation history, model usage, and subscription tier metadata.

Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development — Should Read

Microsoft confirmed it is developing a patch for RoguePlanet, a Microsoft Defender zero-day privilege escalation flaw tracked as CVE-2026-50656 that can grant SYSTEM-level access. The publicly released exploit reportedly works inconsistently across machines and may function even when real-time protection is disabled.

  • Microsoft disclosed that it is working on a patch for a Microsoft Defender zero-day codenamed RoguePlanet.
  • The vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-50656, has a CVSS score of 7.8, and is described by Microsoft as an elevation of privilege flaw in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine.
  • Security researcher Chaotic Eclipse, also known as Nightmare-Eclipse, released RoguePlanet and described it as a race condition that can grant attackers a shell with SYSTEM-level privileges.
  • Chaotic Eclipse said the proof-of-concept exploit works regardless of whether real-time protection is enabled and may also work in passive mode, though passive mode was not tested.
  • RoguePlanet is the fourth Defender vulnerability disclosed by Chaotic Eclipse after BlueHammer, UnDefend, and RedSun, all of which Microsoft has patched.

AdaptixC2: Fingerprinting an Open-Source C2 Framework at Scale — Should Read

AdaptixC2’s default HTTP headers and 404 page make many deployed teamservers and beacon listeners easy to fingerprint at internet scale, with Censys tracking 412 web properties across 236 hosts. Defenders can detect both default and lightly modified deployments using the provided header, body, DNS, and Censys threat-label queries.

  • AdaptixC2’s default configuration sets the HTTP headers `Server: AdaptixC2` and `Adaptix-Version: v1.2` on unauthenticated error responses, making default deployments identifiable through passive scanning.
  • As of 16 June 2026, Censys tracked 412 web properties across 236 hosts running AdaptixC2 with default or near-default settings.
  • Censys identified three beacon listener clusters hosted by Hivelocity, M247, and PSB Hosting that used adjacent IP ranges, randomized or shared high ports, and identical default TLS certificates.
  • One host, 2.26.229[.]254, served AdaptixC2 payloads from an open HTTP directory, including a Linux installer that deployed `timesync.bin` and established 12 persistence mechanisms.
  • The article states that operators who changed the `Server` header could still be detected because they left the default 404 body string, `You need to enter the correct connection details.`, intact.

Socket Firewall Now Blocks Malicious VS Code and Open VSX Extensions — Should Read

Socket Firewall now supports blocking malicious VS Code Marketplace and Open VSX extensions at install and update time, before they can run in developer environments. The move targets growing risks from compromised editor extensions like Nx Console and GlassWorm campaigns.

  • Socket Firewall now supports VS Code Marketplace and Open VSX extension installs to help organizations block malicious editor extensions before they reach developer environments.
  • The article cites GitHub’s May 2026 disclosure that attackers compromised an employee device through a poisoned third-party VS Code extension and exfiltrated roughly 3,800 GitHub-internal repositories.
  • Socket Firewall routes editor marketplace requests through Socket’s proxy, forwards them to the real registry, checks threat verdicts, and filters or blocks flagged extensions during search, install, or update flows.
  • Socket Firewall provides a second enforcement point by checking verdicts before forwarding VSIX downloads from extension asset domains, helping block malicious extensions even if an install flow reaches an underlying download URL.
  • Extension Firewall is available in beta for Socket Enterprise customers and supports marketplace filtering, VSIX download blocking, and deployment through editor configuration, DNS override, or enterprise egress forwarding.

What the ThreatLabz 2026 Phishing and Initial Access Report Means for the Public Sector — Should Read

ThreatLabz says phishing targeting the public sector is becoming more AI-generated, targeted, and capable of bypassing legacy defenses, even as overall volume declines. Government, healthcare, and education should prioritize encrypted traffic inspection, phishing-resistant authentication, reduced exposed attack surface, and broader visibility beyond email.

  • The ThreatLabz 2026 Phishing and Initial Access Report found that overall phishing volume in the Zscaler cloud fell 20% year over year, while remaining campaigns became more targeted, AI-powered, and harder to distinguish from legitimate activity.
  • ThreatLabz identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances during the analysis period and flagged 9% of them as malicious, with platforms such as Manus AI, BlackBox AI, and Anything AI used to create phishing infrastructure quickly.
  • Phishing attempts against the government sector increased 50% year over year, reaching 138.5 million phishing hits in 2025 and making government the third-most targeted industry overall in the Zscaler cloud.
  • Healthcare had comparatively lower phishing hit counts in 2025, but the article states that 95.2% of all phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels and that adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle phishing kits can capture credentials and MFA tokens during active login flows.
  • Phishing attempts against education organizations dropped 65.6% year over year, but education still experienced roughly 1.6 billion encrypted attack hits, representing 6% of encrypted attack activity in the Zscaler cloud.

Crypto Clipper Campaign Abuses Fake Reviews, AI Narrators, and VirusTotal Comments — Should Read

Check Point says an unknown threat actor is using fake reviews, inflated downloads, AI-narrated YouTube tutorials, VirusTotal comments, and syndicated press releases to make malicious crypto tools look trustworthy. The downloads deliver a Rust-based clipboard hijacker for Windows and macOS that swaps copied crypto wallet addresses with attacker-controlled ones.

  • Check Point Research reported that an unknown threat actor is promoting malicious crypto-related tools using paid or promoted posts on legitimate news websites, fake reviews, inflated download counts, and tutorial-style videos.
  • The campaign distributes a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijacker disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots and crash-game predictors.
  • The malware targets Windows and macOS systems and replaces copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses from a hard-coded list.
  • The threat actor uses coordinated accounts on VirusTotal and at least six GitHub accounts to post positive comments, upvotes, stars, and forks that make malicious files and repositories appear trustworthy.
  • The campaign also uses SourceForge, a dedicated YouTube channel with AI-generated narrators, a WordPress phishing page, and EIN Presswire press release syndication to promote the malicious tools.

Operationalize CISA BOD 26-04 with Tenable One — May Read

CISA BOD 26-04 pushes federal agencies away from static CVSS-based patching toward continuous, risk-based vulnerability prioritization using asset exposure, KEV status, exploit automation, and technical impact. Tenable argues that Tenable One can operationalize these requirements through continuous asset visibility, threat intelligence, cloud exposure management, and AI-driven automation.

  • CISA BOD 26-04 supersedes BOD 19-02 and BOD 22-01 and shifts federal vulnerability management from static severity-score prioritization to dynamic, risk-based prioritization using real-world asset and threat context.
  • The directive bases remediation timelines on four risk variables: asset exposure, KEV status, exploit automation, and technical impact.
  • Tenable One is presented as a platform that maps to the directive’s risk variables through continuous asset discovery, attack surface management, KEV integration, Vulnerability Priority Rating, and technical impact context.
  • The article states that BOD 26-04 introduces a forensic triage requirement for KEV vulnerabilities that yield total system control, alongside three-day remediation for the highest-severity cases.
  • Tenable says Tenable Hexa AI, API integrations, automated asset tagging, and CDM Dashboard integration can help agencies meet the directive’s phased implementation requirements and metadata-tagging obligations.

The Invoice Portal Link That Didn't Need a Password — May Read

A fully authenticated invoice email from a legitimate Ecocert accounting subdomain used a tokenized Upflow billing portal link that granted passwordless access via a long-lived JWT. The key risk is that valid authentication and clean URLs can still expose billing portals and enable invoice fraud when access tokens are embedded directly in emailed links.

  • A French-language invoice notification was sent from comptabilitegreenlife@accounting.ecocert.com through Mailgun and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication with compauth=100.
  • The email linked to an Upflow customer billing portal through a redirect on upflow-email.accounting.ecocert.com, and the destination URL contained a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration.
  • Anyone possessing the tokenized URL could access the billing portal without entering credentials.
  • The attached PDF invoice, Facture-FR03IN26001665.pdf, requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC payment details.
  • A 1x1 tracking pixel from the upflow-email subdomain confirmed mailbox activity, and Themis quarantined four affected mailboxes after flagging the invoice phishing pattern at 71% confidence.

Junior Hacker Used Tailscale and OpenSSH to Keep Access After His C2 Went Offline — May Read

A low-skill attacker maintained access to a French small business even after his Havoc C2 went offline by installing OpenSSH and Tailscale as a separate backdoor. The key lesson is that taking down C2 infrastructure is not enough remediation unless defenders also hunt for alternate persistence paths like legitimate remote-access tools, scheduled tasks, and keyloggers.

  • A French-speaking attacker known as "Poisson" compromised a small French automotive business, installed a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials.
  • Before his Havoc command-and-control server went offline, Poisson installed OpenSSH Server and Tailscale on a victim machine to maintain access outside the C2 channel.
  • Cato Networks captured 339 commands over 33 days after the operator left SSH keys and a step-by-step playbook in an open storage bucket.
  • The intrusion used a VBScript stager, PowerShell loader, .NET loader, Havoc Demon agent, scheduled-task persistence, Explorer.exe injection, RustDesk, and a Python keylogger.
  • Cato Networks recommended hunting for OpenSSH Server on Windows workstations, tailscale.exe on unexpected machines, ssh -R reverse tunnels, suspicious wscript.exe activity, high-privilege scheduled tasks launching script interpreters, powercfg standby-timeout changes, and DuckDNS usage.

Google to use UK and EU user IP addresses for ad personalization — May Read

Google will begin using UK, EEA, and Swiss users’ IP addresses for ad measurement and personalization from around August 3, 2026, making IP-based device identification part of its ad targeting in regions where IP addresses are regulated as personal data. The move raises consent and fingerprinting concerns under UK and EU privacy rules, with Google placing responsibility on advertisers to obtain valid user consent.

  • Google has notified advertisers that it will start using IP addresses for ad measurement and personalization across the EEA, the UK, and Switzerland on or shortly after August 3, 2026.
  • The change means IP addresses that Google already receives for routing traffic and delivering ads will also be used to identify devices for measurement and ad personalization.
  • Google will register under IAB Europe’s Transparency and Consent Framework for Feature 3, which covers identifying devices based on information transmitted automatically, including IP addresses.
  • The article says IP addresses are personal data under GDPR in the EEA, the UK, and Switzerland, and that using them to identify a device is a building block of fingerprinting.
  • Google’s customer email says advertisers remain bound by its EU User Consent Policy and must obtain valid consent from users in the affected regions.

Microsoft confirms Office apps launch issues after June updates — May Read

Microsoft says Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026 can prevent some third-party apps using OLE automation from launching Office apps or opening Office documents. There is no fix yet, but users can work around it by opening Office apps or documents directly while Microsoft prepares a future Windows update.

  • Microsoft is investigating an issue where certain third-party applications cannot launch Microsoft Office applications or open documents after installing Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026.
  • The issue affects Office applications including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Access, and other Microsoft Office apps when launched from impacted third-party applications.
  • Microsoft said the problem affects certain third-party applications that use OLE automation to interact with Microsoft Office applications, and the Office app or document may fail to open without an error message.
  • User reports identify affected third-party applications including CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, Softdent, and similar applications.
  • Microsoft advised affected users to open Office applications or documents directly as a workaround while it works on a resolution for a future Windows update.

EU grants Ukraine access to cybersecurity reserve for major attacks — May Read

Ukraine has been granted access to the EU Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing it to request EU-approved experts for major cyberattacks that exceed its own response capacity. The move deepens EU-Ukraine security cooperation and integrates Kyiv further into Europe’s collective cyber defense framework.

  • Ukraine has been granted access to the European Union's Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing Kyiv to call on EU-approved experts during major cyberattacks.
  • The EU Cybersecurity Reserve is managed by ENISA and provides incident response, technical expertise, and recovery support to participating countries facing cyber incidents beyond their own capabilities.
  • Ukraine will be able to request European assistance for digital forensics, incident response, and system recovery if a cyberattack exceeds the capacity of its own teams.
  • Natalia Tkachuk said Ukraine already shares intelligence on Russian hacking techniques with European partners and participates in joint cybercrime investigations and attribution efforts.
  • Ukraine hopes its cybersecurity companies will eventually be able to join the Cyber Reserve as trusted service providers under the EU's Cyber Solidarity Act.

Warner warns of CISA cuts, staffing gaps in letter to acting chief — May Read

Sen. Mark Warner warned CISA’s acting director that major staff cuts, leadership vacancies, regional staffing gaps and loss of MS-ISAC funding are undermining the agency’s ability to support state and local critical infrastructure cybersecurity. He is pressing DHS and CISA for staffing, service-delivery and hiring details while urging renewed funding for the MS-ISAC.

  • Sen. Mark Warner sent CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen a letter expressing alarm over widespread agency cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators.
  • The article says the administration has cut about one-third of CISA’s staff and proposed more than $700 million in cuts to CISA’s fiscal year 2027 budget.
  • Warner said state and local officials, industry leaders, law enforcement, education advocates, and cybersecurity experts have reported reduced responsiveness and support from CISA amid staffing turbulence.
  • CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen recently announced that CISA is hiring more than 300 additional workers, with some already having started.
  • Warner asked CISA to provide organizational charts, vacancy explanations, regional service metrics, and details on how many new hires will be assigned to regional jobs and what experience they have.

I’m Just Asking Questions: Social Engineering as a Reporter — May Read

NetSPI describes a social engineering test where impersonating a journalist with an urgent media inquiry led an executive to engage with a phishing link and even forward it to vendors. The key lesson is that organizations need clear, simple procedures for handling unsolicited sensitive inquiries, especially for executives, rather than relying only on individual vigilance.

  • NetSPI conducted an authorized social engineering assessment to test how vulnerable a client’s executive leadership team was to targeted manipulation.
  • The testers used open-source research about the client’s planned new facility to create a journalist impersonation pretext involving alleged hazardous waste disposal at the construction site.
  • NetSPI created a ProtonMail account in a journalist’s name, registered a lookalike domain, and deployed an Evilginx server to mimic the client’s Microsoft login flow and capture authentication data during the test.
  • An executive overseeing the construction project responded to the pretext, received a phishing link after a claimed Teams error, and forwarded the link to two contracting firms involved in the project.
  • NetSPI stopped the phishing session and notified its point of contact because the contracting firms were outside the engagement scope.

The Top 10 Attack Surface Exposures in 2026 — May Read

Intruder’s analysis of 3,000 attack surfaces found that many organizations are exposing unnecessary internet-facing assets, including databases, admin panels, API docs, and legacy services. The key takeaway is that reducing what is publicly reachable is as important as patching, especially as exploitation timelines shrink.

  • Intruder analyzed 3,000 attack surfaces and found that 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed.
  • Nearly half of organizations had a risky port or service exposed, 42% had a database reachable directly from the internet, and 30% had files or information publicly accessible that should not have been.
  • The most common exposure was MySQL databases at 26%, followed by Postgres databases at 16%, API documentation at 15%, and WordPress admin panels at 15%.
  • API documentation ranked third among exposures, ahead of Remote Desktop Service, which ranked fifth at 11%.
  • The article states that services such as SNMP, UPnP, NTP, and RPC were designed for internal networks and were never meant to be internet-facing.

Why Native AI Guardrails Fail SecOp — Optional

Native AI guardrails from model providers are often too generic, opaque, and provider-focused to meet enterprise SecOps needs, creating both usability friction and gaps in data protection, misuse prevention, and visibility. Organizations should deploy their own tailored guardrails to enforce consistent, observable, and predictable controls across users, models, and AI tools.

  • The article argues that native AI guardrails from providers such as Amazon Bedrock, Anthropic, and OpenAI are typically designed to protect the provider’s liability and intellectual property rather than an organization’s SecOps requirements.
  • Provider guardrails can create friction for cybersecurity researchers by blocking legitimate security-related work while still failing to prevent intellectual property or sensitive prompts from being sent to the provider.
  • The article says organizations need different guardrail policies for different user groups, such as cybersecurity researchers, HR staff, and software engineers using Claude Code.
  • Relying on provider guardrails can create observability gaps because refusals or silent degradation often do not provide clear logs showing which guardrail was triggered or why.
  • The article presents Netskope One AI Guardrails as a way to apply granular policies, protect organizational assets, provide uniform controls across models, create audit trails, and scan or redact sensitive data before it reaches third-party AI providers.

Why Account Takeovers Are Rising and How to Stop Them — Optional

Account takeover attacks are rising because attackers increasingly steal credentials, hijack sessions, bypass MFA, and exploit unmanaged or compromised devices. The article argues organizations need continuous identity and device trust checks throughout sessions, not just authentication at login.

  • Account takeover attacks are increasing because organizations manage many human and non-human identities across cloud services, SaaS applications, endpoints, and remote environments while losing visibility into access and trust.
  • Attackers are adapting to multi-factor authentication by using MFA fatigue, adversary-in-the-middle frameworks, and session hijacking tools to steal authenticated session tokens or gain approval.
  • Credential phishing remains common and has become more sophisticated through legitimate hosting services, trusted domains, reverse proxies, and AI-generated content that mimic real login portals.
  • Personal laptops, unmanaged mobile devices, and compromised endpoints expand the attack surface by giving attackers opportunities to harvest credentials, browser-stored passwords, and authenticated session cookies.
  • The article says organizations need continuous verification of device posture, session risk, and behavioral signals throughout the access lifecycle rather than relying only on successful authentication.

Built for MSP SASE Growth: Introducing Netskope Catalyst Program — Optional

Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program to help managed service providers onboard SASE customers faster, manage multi-tenant environments, and improve margins. The program includes tiered benefits, self-service tenant provisioning, training, support, pricing incentives, and go-to-market resources.

  • Netskope introduced the Catalyst MSP/SP Program to help service providers run managed SSE and SASE businesses across multiple customers at scale.
  • The program has three partner tiers, Foundation, Ascent, and Summit, which unlock progressively greater benefits across six pillars.
  • Netskope Partner Orchestrator allows partners to create new customer tenants in under 15 minutes through a self-service portal and provides license portability and granular multi-tenant access controls.
  • Catalyst includes tiered programmatic discounts, no upfront financial commitments for managed offerings, and 24×7 MSP support without required add-ons.
  • The program offers expanded training and accreditations, Market Development Funds, co-branded demand generation programs, service creation workshops, a unified partner portal, and deal protection for registered opportunities.

Leak confirms OpenAI is testing a ChatGPT for Science subscription — Optional

OpenAI appears to be testing a new “ChatGPT for Science” subscription aimed at scientific and research use cases, likely with access restricted to verified institutions or universities. The offering may bring some specialized capabilities from OpenAI’s science-focused models to a broader set of research organizations.

  • OpenAI appears to be testing a new subscription and experience called ChatGPT for Science, with references found in the web build.
  • The article says it is unclear whether ChatGPT for Science will be available to everyone regardless of their background.
  • OpenAI currently offers ChatGPT for personal use, Teams, and business or enterprise customers.
  • The article says ChatGPT for Science will likely have restrictions and may be limited to verified institutes or universities.
  • OpenAI recently announced GPT-Rosalind, a specialized model for enterprise-scale life sciences research available through a trusted-access deployment structure.

What Is the Preparation Phase? The Part of the Attack Timeline Most Tools Miss — Optional

The article argues that most security tools detect threats too late because attackers spend weeks or months preparing domains, servers, DNS, and certificates before any malicious activity appears. Silent Push promotes its preemptive approach for identifying this staged infrastructure before campaigns launch.

  • The preparation phase is the period when a threat actor builds and stages attack infrastructure such as domains, servers, DNS records, certificates, and testing environments before a campaign goes live.
  • Conventional tools such as SIEMs, EDR platforms, blocklists, and standard threat intelligence feeds depend on observed malicious events or known-bad indicators, so they usually do not detect preparation-phase activity.
  • The article states that traditional threat data captures roughly the final 5% of the offensive lifecycle, when infrastructure is already operational and a malicious campaign is already running.
  • Silent Push says its platform maps internet infrastructure across DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, and host configurations to generate Indicators of Future Attack for staging environments before they are used.
  • The article claims Silent Push provides an average early-detection lead time of 104 days ahead of a traditional SIEM alert, with some APT campaigns exceeding 300 days.

Adversarial Exposure Validation Turns Security Visibility into Confident Prioritization — Optional

Security teams already have ample visibility into vulnerabilities, but the real challenge is validating which exposures are actually exploitable and business-critical. BreachLock argues that Adversarial Exposure Validation helps turn overwhelming findings into confident remediation priorities by combining realistic attack validation with human expertise.

  • The article argues that security teams now have extensive visibility into their environments, but they struggle to determine which findings warrant action first.
  • The article states that organizations need to distinguish between theoretical exposure and practical, exploitable risk in order to prioritize remediation effectively.
  • Adversarial Exposure Validation is presented as a component of Continuous Threat Exposure Management that validates which exposures are reachable, exploitable, and consequential in an organization’s environment.
  • The article says AI can help with discovery, scale, signal processing, and analysis, but cannot independently solve prioritization decisions that require business context and human judgment.
  • The article concludes that confidence in prioritization is an operational security capability that helps teams communicate risk clearly and invest resources where they reduce the most exposure.

item.title: Mastra npm Compromise, RoguePlanet CVE-2026-50656, and AI Tool Data Theft
item.description: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege zero-day and a confirmed Kodak data breach claimed by ShinyHunters. Multiple reports from Aikido, Socket, and The Hacker News describe a coordinated npm supply chain attack affecting more than 140 packages in the @mastra namespace. The malicious publications occurred on June 17, when a hijacked npm account belonging to a legitimate former Mastra contributor mass-published compromised versions across the scope within a short window. The affected packages reportedly did not embed malware directly, but instead added a dependency on easy-day-js, a malicious clone or typosquat of dayjs. The easy-day-js package used a postinstall hook to execute an obfuscated setup script during installation. That script downloaded a second-stage payload from attacker-controlled infrastructure, including 23[.]254[.]164[.]92:8000, wrote it to a randomly named JavaScript file in the operating system temporary directory, spawned it as a detached background process, and deleted itself. Socket reported that the loader also disabled TLS certificate validation before retrieving the payload. The recovered second-stage malware was described as a cross-platform Node.js tasking client with persistence mechanisms for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Its collection logic targeted host reconnaissance data, browser history from Chrome, Edge, and Brave, and more than 160 cryptocurrency wallet browser extensions. Researchers noted similarities to an earlier axios compromise, including the use of a malicious dependency, a clean decoy version preceding the malicious release, automatic postinstall execution, and self-deleting droppers. Aikido Security reported a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 malicious plugins masquerading as AI coding assistants. The plugins reportedly functioned as advertised while secretly exfiltrating AI provider API keys entered by users, including keys for OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek. The stolen keys were sent over plaintext HTTP to 39.107.60[.]51. Two of the reported JetBrains plugins, CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist, were listed with more than 25,000 downloads each, although the authenticity of those counts was unclear. The campaign reflects continued attacker interest in developer environments where AI service credentials may be stored, entered, or reused across workflows. Separately, researcher Jean-Marie R. reported that two Chrome ad blocker extensions, Smart Adblocker and Adblock for Browser, captured users’ conversations with AI chatbots. The extensions reportedly provided genuine ad-blocking capability while operating an undisclosed telemetry channel that collected full AI conversation histories, model usage, and subscription tier metadata across services including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity, DeepSeek, Grok, and Meta AI. Microsoft confirmed it is working on a security update for a Microsoft Defender zero-day vulnerability publicly referred to as RoguePlanet and now tracked as CVE-2026-50656. The issue is described as an elevation-of-privilege flaw in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine involving a race condition. Researcher Nightmare Eclipse published a proof-of-concept exploit in a self-hosted Git repository and claimed the exploit affects fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 systems. According to the reporting, the exploit can spawn command prompts with SYSTEM privileges and may work regardless of whether Defender real-time protection is enabled. The disclosure is also tied to an ongoing dispute between the researcher and Microsoft regarding bug bounty and vulnerability disclosure practices. Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party temporarily accessed a limited amount of company data. The company said it engaged external cybersecurity experts, is investigating what data was accessed and copied, and is working with law enforcement. Kodak also stated that it is confident there is no threat to its systems or operations. The ShinyHunters extortion group claimed responsibility for the incident on its dark web leak site. The group alleged it stole more than 2.2 million records containing customer personally identifiable information and internal corporate data, and threatened to leak the data on June 18, 2026.

item.title: Mastra npm Supply Chain Attack Backdoors 140+ Packages via easy-day-js
item.description: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the @mastra npm organization, exposing downstream development and CI/CD environments to a postinstall-based payload chain. The incident is notable for its scale, its targeting of an AI framework package namespace, and its use of a staged delivery mechanism designed to execute during package installation. The affected Mastra packages reportedly had combined weekly downloads exceeding 1.1 million, making this a high-impact software supply chain event for organizations consuming JavaScript and AI development dependencies. StepSecurity reported that on June 17, 2026, an attacker compromised the @mastra npm organization and added a dependency named easy-day-js across more than 140 Mastra packages. The package name closely resembled the legitimate dayjs library, indicating a typosquatting approach intended to blend into JavaScript dependency trees and exploit developer familiarity with common date/time libraries. The malicious package was staged across versions. Version 1.11.21 of easy-day-js was reportedly published as a clean bait package, while version 1.11.22 introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper. This sequencing is consistent with supply chain tradecraft in which an attacker first establishes a benign-looking package history before pushing a malicious update after trust or dependency inclusion has been achieved. The reported malicious behavior centered on npm’s postinstall lifecycle hook. According to StepSecurity, the obfuscated script attempted to download a second-stage payload from https://23.254.164.92:8000/update/49890878, spawn it as a detached Node.js process, and then delete setup.cjs after execution. This design indicates an effort to move beyond package-level code execution into a more persistent or independently running payload while reducing local forensic artifacts tied to the installer script. The use of a direct IP address and nonstandard port for payload retrieval suggests attacker-controlled infrastructure rather than a commodity package registry-hosted payload. StepSecurity also reported that, during a controlled GitHub Actions test, its Harden Runner in block mode intercepted and blocked the outbound connection to 23.254.164.92:8000, preventing the stage-two payload from being retrieved in that environment. The reported scale of the compromised package set places this incident among high-concern npm ecosystem supply chain events, particularly because it affected an AI framework namespace with broad downstream usage. StepSecurity stated that the affected Mastra packages had more than 1.1 million combined weekly downloads, and assessed that anyone who installed an @mastra package on the day of the compromise should consider the environment compromised. For threat hunters and detection engineers, the reporting provides concrete infrastructure and behavioral details: the package name easy-day-js, malicious version 1.11.22, postinstall execution, retrieval from 23.254.164.92:8000, and execution of a detached Node.js second stage. The incident reinforces ongoing adversary interest in developer ecosystems, CI/CD workflows, and trusted open-source dependency chains as distribution mechanisms for code execution at scale.
